data.title: AppLovin confirms intent to buy TikTok
data.description:
Marketing platform AppLovin (NASDAQ:APP) on Thursday confirmed that it has provided “an indication of interest” to the Trump administration to purchase of TikTok in all markets outside of China.  

“This indication of interest is preliminary and there can be no assurance that a transaction involving the Company will proceed,” the company said in a filing.

This comes after reports earlier Wednesday said that Amazon (AMZN) made a last minute bid for the Chinese-owned short-video app.  According to a Reuters report, OnlyFans founder Tim Stokely's startup has submitted an eleventh-hour bid along with a cryptocurrency foundation to buy TikTok U.S.

Other companies interested in buying or taking a stake in TikTok also include Oracle (ORCL), Rumble (RUM), and Perplexity AI.

In 2020, Trump had called for a ban on TikTok and attempted to force its sale to an American company because of its Chinese ownership.

data.title: Stay defensive during upheaval in stocks ignited by Trump tariff plan, Bahnsen says
data.description:
The S&P 500’s (SP500) Consumer Staples sector (NYSEARCA:XLP) was the lone gainer in Thursday’s stock rout, a move in line with a suggestion from The Bahnsen Group’s leader to focus on defensive areas as markets digest the Trump administration’s tariff plans.  

Trump late Wednesday said the U.S. would implement a minimum baseline 10% tariff on imports worldwide, with some rates sharply higher. The S&P 500 (SP500) sank -4.8%, but Consumer Staples(NYSEARCA:XLP) rose +0.7%, outperforming the benchmark’s (SP500) ten other sectors. 

“Our main message for investors is to stay defensive by focusing on stocks that have strong cash flows, a long history of growing their dividend, and ones that can withstand any kind of economic environment,” David Bahnsen, chief investment officer of The Bahnsen Group, which oversees $7B in assets under management, said in a Thursday note. 

Verizon (NYSE:VZ), Merck (NYSE:MRK), and Procter & Gamble (NYSE:PG) are defensive stocks to consider, Bahnsen said. Meanwhile, the “riskiest and most expensive parts of the market,” including Big Tech, many of AI-related names and consumer discretionary sectors, will be hit in the current risk-off environment, he said. 

A "Q2 or Q3 recession is very possible, as is a bear market,” if the current slate of tariffs hold, Bahnsen said.

“The question is, ‘Does President Trump seek some sort of off-ramp for these policies if and when we see a bear market in the stock market?,” the CIO said. “We believe Trump will then pivot to focus on the number of companies that are making significant investments in the U.S., but it's unclear that would reverse market sentiment.” 

Verizon (NYSE:VZ) rose 2% on Thursday, and Merck (NYSE:MRK) slipped 0.2%. The ~$17B Consumer Staples Select Sector SPDR ETF (XLP) gained +0.6%. Here’s how its 10 biggest holdings performed Thursday: 

  • Costco (NASDAQ:COST): +0.2%
  • Procter & Gamble (NYSE:PG): +1.7%
  • Walmart (NYSE:WMT): -2.8%
  • Coca-Cola (NYSE:KO): +2.6%
  • Philip Morris (NYSE:PM): +3.8%
  • PepsiCo (NASDAQ:PEP): +1.5%
  • Colgate-Palmolive (NYSE:CL): +2.6%
  • Mondelez International (NASDAQ:MDLZ): +2.7%
  • Altria (NYSE:MO): +1.4%
  • Target (NYSE:TGT): -10.9% 

Other Consumer Staples ETFs include: (VDC), (IYK), (FSTA), (KXI), and (RSPS).

Dear readers: We recognize that politics often intersects with the financial news of the day, so we invite you to click here to join the separate political discussion.

data.title: Goldman Sachs flags tariff risks for industrials ahead of Q1 earnings
data.description:
As the first quarter earnings season approaches, Goldman Sachs is advising investors to tread carefully amid rising concerns over tariffs and slowing short-cycle momentum, particularly for multi-industry companies.

In a recent note, the bank highlighted that ongoing uncertainty around U.S. trade policy—especially the potential non-renewal of the U.S.-Mexico-Canada Agreement — is creating volatility across the industrial landscape. Companies with strong ties to Mexico, such as Stanley Black & Decker (NYSE:SWK), Carrier (NYSE:CARR), Lennox (NYSE:LII) and Regal Rexnord (NYSE:RRX), could face heightened risks if existing trade protections are not extended.

"The level of transparency on tariff exposure varies widely, but Mexico appears to carry the most incremental risk," Joe Ritchie, analyst at Goldman, said in an April 1 report. 

Beyond tariffs, another major concern for industrials is the apparent stagnation in short-cycle demand. According to Goldman Sachs' recent channel checks and attendance at the ProMat trade show, few companies have seen significant shifts in short-cycle activity since new tariffs were announced. However, the firm cautions that prolonged uncertainty could eventually weigh on demand — particularly in the second half of 2025, when several companies have projected stronger performance.

Despite these macro headwinds, Goldman is optimistic about select names heading into Q1 earnings. The firm sees the strongest near-term potential in General Electric Vernova (NYSE:GEV), Dover (NYSE:DOV), Honeywell (NASDAQ:HON) and Johnson Controls (NYSE:JCI) — each supported by solid order momentum or unique catalysts.

  • GE Vernova (NYSE:GEV): With a target price of $500, Goldman forecasts first-quarter ebitda of $360 million, slightly above consensus, and maintains the stock on its Americas Conviction List.

  • Dover (NYSE:DOV): The company’s steady order growth has continued into Q1, and Goldman expects a notable acceleration in organic growth for Q2, potentially lifting the stock.

  • Honeywell International (NASDAQ:HON): Following strong Q4 orders and a solid start to the year, Goldman believes Honeywell is positioned to resume a “beat and raise” pattern in 2025.

  • Johnson Controls (NYSE:JCI): While Q2 results are expected to be in line, investor sentiment could benefit from the first public remarks by the company’s new CEO.

On the flip side, the firm issued a more cautious outlook for companies with high exposure to tariffs or heavy reliance on original equipment manufacturer channels.

  • Stanley Black & Decker (NYSE:SWK): Facing the highest tariff exposure in Goldman’s coverage, the company may struggle to meet guidance as it contends with incremental trade headwinds from China and Mexico.

  • Regal Rexnord (NYSE:RRX): Though rated a Buy, Goldman sees downside risk to the firm’s full-year EPS forecast due to its unmitigated $60 million tariff impact.

  • Timken (NYSE:TKR): With over half of its sales tied to OEM customers and ongoing leadership uncertainty, the firm sees limited upside for the stock in the near term.

Goldman also noted that it has adjusted earnings estimates for certain companies based on recent 10-K and 10-Q filings, closed M&A transactions, and management commentary from recent industry conferences.

Looking further ahead, Goldman remains bullish on longer-term structural themes such as power infrastructure and electrification. Stocks like Eaton (NYSE:ETN), GE Vernova (GEV) and nVent Electric (NYSE:NVT) are well positioned to benefit from these trends, according to the firm.

data.title: Steel, aluminum names fall sharply as Trump tariffs some steel inputs, spares others
data.description:
Imported steel and aluminum already subject to a 25% tariff are excluded from President Trump's new levies, providing some relief for metal buyers that would have faced significantly higher prices, but the new tariffs nevertheless will raise the cost of raw materials for steelmakers that operate electric arc furnaces, Argus reports.

Shares of major steel and aluminum producers plunged in Thursday's trading: Cleveland-Cliffs (NYSE:CLF) -16.8%, Commecial Metals (CMC) -11.5%, Alcoa (AA) -11.4%, Constellium (CSTM) -11.2%, Nucor (NYSE:NUE) -10.7%, Kaier Aluminum (KALU) -10.6%, Steel Dynamics (STLD) -8.8%, Ternium. (TX) -8.5%, US Steel (X) -8.1%, ArcelorMittal (MT) -7.3%, Reliance Steel (RS) -6.4%, Century Aluminum (CENX) -6.1%.

Pig iron from Brazil, direct reduced iron from Trinidad, and ferrous scrap from the U.K. will face 10% tariffs, and ferrous scrap imports from the European Union will take a 20% levy, according to Argus.

Two notable exceptions from the announced tariffs are scrap from Mexico and Canada, although separate 25% tariffs on Canadian steel, aluminum and automobiles remain in effect.

Trump's 10% tariffs on imports from Brazil and other pig iron producing countries could drive up costs for U.S. steelmakers, especially those with electric arc furnaces, Argus says.

The 10% levy also will apply to Nucor's (NYSE:NUE) direct reduced iron plant in Trinidad and Tobago, Argus says; Nucor, the largest EAF steelmaker in the U.S., imports ~125K tons of DRI each month from its Trinidad plant.

U.S. hot-rolled coil prices already have climbed 22% since Trump announced the 25% steel tariffs in early February, and the tariffs on iron metallics announced Wednesday could cause steelmakers to raise their steel selling prices even more, Argus says.

ETF: (SLX)

data.title: Eli Lilly to leverage Sangamo capsid technology for CNS disease therapies
data.description:
• Sangamo Therapeutics (NASDAQ:SGMO) has agreed to license its neurotropic adeno-associated virus (AAV) capsid, STAC-BBB, to Eli Lilly (NYSE:LLY) for one target to deliver genomic medicine to treat central nervous system diseases.
  • Sangamo is up ~28% in after-hours trading Thursday.
  • The agreement also allows Lilly to add up to an additional four targets upon paying additional fees.
  • The arrangement is third one Sangamo has done with a pharma since STAC-BBB was discovered in March 2024. Those were made with Roche's (OTCQX:RHHBY) Genentech unit and Astellas Pharma (OTCPK:ALPMF)(OTCPK:ALPMY). 
  • Terms of the deal call for Sangamo to receive an $18M upfront license fee from Lilly and is eligible for up to $1.4B in additional licensed target fees and milestone payments across the up to five neurology disease targets, plus tiered royalties on any marketed products.

data.title: PAMT CORP commences tender offer to purchase up to 435,000 shares
data.description:
• PAMT CORP (NASDAQ:PAMT) announced on Thursday the commencement of a modified “Dutch auction” tender offer to purchase up to 435,000 shares, or about 2.0%, of its outstanding common stock at a price of not less than $14.00 and not more than $17.00 per share.
  • The tender offer will expire at 5:00 p.m., Eastern Time, on May 1, 2025, unless extended or withdrawn.
  • The Company anticipates paying for the shares tendered in the offer from its available cash and cash equivalents, but may utilize funds borrowed under its existing line of credit.
  • A modified “Dutch auction” tender offer allows stockholders to indicate how many shares and at what price(s) they wish to tender their shares within the specified price range.
  • Based on the number of shares tendered, and the prices specified by the tendering stockholders, the company will determine the lowest price per share within the range that will allow it to purchase up to 435,000 shares of its common stock, or a lower amount depending on the number of shares properly tendered and not properly withdrawn.
  • PAMT +3.78% after hours to $11.53
  • Source: Press Release

data.title: Ducommun expects minimal impact from tariffs
data.description:
• Ducommun (NYSE:DCO) on Thursday said the tariffs are expected to have a minimal impact to its operations or supply base.
  • The company reaffirmed its commitment to American manufacturing with 95% of its revenue derived from operations based in the United States.
  • Source: Press Release

data.title: Deloitte plans job cuts in U.S. advisory services amid federal pressure: WSJ
data.description:
Deloitte is set to reduce its U.S. workforce, particularly within its government consulting and advisory divisions, in response to federal efforts to identify savings in existing contracts, The Wall Street Journal reported Thursday.

A spokesperson for the firm described the move as a “modest” adjustment, citing slower growth in specific sectors, shifting demands from government clients, and fewer employees voluntarily leaving their positions. Despite the planned cuts, the company maintains that overall demand for its services remains robust.

Employees in the U.S. consulting branch were informed Thursday that notifications to those impacted will roll out in the coming weeks.

Deloitte’s U.S. management consulting division reported a growth of just 0.7% in 2024 — a significant drop from 17.8% in 2023 and 25.5% in 2022. Meanwhile, financial advisory revenues declined by 0.5% in 2024, after increasing 3.7% the year prior and 11% in 2022.

While Deloitte hasn't disclosed how many roles will be eliminated, the company’s U.S. headcount stood at nearly 173,000 at the close of 2024.

The move comes as Deloitte and other major federal contractors face pressure from the General Services Administration to reassess and reduce contract costs. Several firms have proposed cutting billions in expenditures, but sources say the GSA is pushing for even deeper reductions, questioning the initial proposals.

Many accounting firms are experiencing lower-than-anticipated turnover after a hiring surge during the pandemic. As a result, recent layoffs across the industry have focused on advisory roles, where demand has waned. Deloitte previously cut 1.5% of its U.S. staff in 2023, the Journal reported.

data.title: Riot Platforms produces 13% more bitcoin in March vs. February
data.description:
Riot Platforms (NASDAQ:RIOT) mined 533 bitcoin (BTC-USD) in March, up 13% from the 470 generated in February and 25% from the 425 produced a year ago, marking "a new post-halving production high for the company," CEO Jason Les said in a statement Thursday.

Average BTC produced each day of the month was 17.2 tokens, up from 16.8 in February and 13.7 in March 2024.

Its deployed hash rate ticked up to 33.7 exahashes per second (EH/s) from 33.6 EH/s in the earlier month and climbed from 12.4 EH/s in March 2024.

"The operational improvements our onsite teams have made are demonstrating results in both operating hash rate growth and bitcoin production, despite increases in the network difficulty," Les said.

In the meantime, total power credits totaled $0.9M, compared with $2.8M in February and $1.2M in the year-ago period.

RIOT shares edged down 0.4% in after-hours trading.
